meta.title: Best Rowing Machine 2026: 5 Tested Picks for Home Gyms
meta.description: From the Concept2 Model D to budget magnetic rowers — our tested picks for the best rowing machine of 2026.

lede: Rowing hits back, legs, core, and arms simultaneously — making it one of the most efficient full-body cardio machines you can own. These five rowers cover every budget and training style.

sections.heading: Concept2 Model D: The Undisputed Standard
sections.body: Used in Olympic training facilities and commercial gyms worldwide, the Concept2 Model D sets the baseline for rowing quality. Air resistance provides an infinitely variable, self-regulating challenge — the harder you pull, the harder it gets. The PM5 monitor tracks splits, watts, and heart rate with precision that serious athletes rely on. Folds vertically to roughly 25×33 inches, storing against a wall. At roughly $900, it's not cheap, but it outlasts cheaper machines by decades.

sections.heading: Hydrow Wave: Live Classes on a Compact Footprint
sections.body: The Wave is Hydrow's smaller, more affordable entry point — electromagnetic resistance with a 16-inch touchscreen streaming live and on-demand rows with professional athletes. The feel is smoother than air, quieter for apartment living, and the workout library is genuinely motivating. Subscription required (~$44/month) for full content access. Folds to save floor space. A strong choice if connected fitness keeps you consistent.

sections.heading: NordicTrack RW900: The 22-Inch Screen Experience
sections.body: NordicTrack's flagship rower brings a massive 22-inch HD rotating touchscreen and iFIT integration with global workout routes. The 26 digital resistance levels adjust automatically during workouts. Heavier than competitors at 218 lbs, so placement is semi-permanent. iFIT subscription ($39/month) unlocks the full library. If you want the biggest screen and the most guided content options, the RW900 delivers.

sections.heading: Ergatta: Water Resistance Meets Gamified Fitness
sections.body: Ergatta uses an actual water flywheel for the most natural rowing feel — the sound and resistance mimic open-water rowing convincingly. The workout approach is game-based rather than instructor-led: race against past performances, compete in challenges, or follow structured programs. The 17.3-inch touchscreen runs Ergatta's proprietary platform ($29/month). Best for solo athletes who find instructor workouts tedious but need engagement to stay consistent.

sections.heading: Sunny Health SF-RW5515: Best Budget Magnetic Rower
sections.body: At around $350, the SF-RW5515 offers 12 levels of magnetic resistance, a respectable aluminum rail, and a simple LCD monitor. It won't match the feel of air or water rowers, but it's quiet, smooth, and genuinely functional for building a rowing habit. The 43.3-inch slide means it's designed for users up to 6'3". No subscription required — just row. For beginners testing whether rowing is right for them, this is the low-risk starting point.

faqs.question: Air, water, or magnetic resistance — which is best?
faqs.answer: Air resistance (Concept2) provides the most realistic feel and scales infinitely with effort. Water resistance (Ergatta) is close to air but quieter and more atmospheric. Magnetic resistance (Sunny, Hydrow Wave) is the quietest and best for apartments. Connected rowers often use electromagnetic resistance for programmatic control during instructor workouts.

faqs.question: How much space does a rowing machine need?
faqs.answer: Most full-size rowers need roughly 8–9 feet of length and 2 feet of width during use. The Concept2 Model D and many others fold vertically to ~25×33 inches for storage. Measure your ceiling height if storing upright — you'll need at least 6.5 feet.

faqs.question: Is rowing good for weight loss?
faqs.answer: Rowing is highly effective for calorie burn — a 30-minute session can burn 250–400 calories depending on intensity, comparable to running but with less joint impact. The full-body muscle engagement also builds lean mass, which raises resting metabolic rate over time.

offerNotes.concept2-model-d: Also available as the Model E with a higher seat height. Both use the same PM5 monitor.
offerNotes.hydrow-wave-rower: The original Hydrow has a larger 22-inch screen; the Wave is the compact, more affordable version.
offerNotes.nordictrack-rw900: iFIT subscription required for full functionality. 30-day free trial included with purchase.
offerNotes.ergatta-rower: Built on a WaterRower chassis with Ergatta's technology layer. Water flywheel is self-regulating.
offerNotes.sunny-sf-rw5515: No subscription required. Basic LCD monitor tracks time, count, calories, and scan mode.

pinDescription: 5 best rowing machines of 2026 tested — Concept2 vs Hydrow vs NordicTrack vs water vs budget. Full-body cardio at home! 🚣
methodology: Each machine was evaluated on resistance feel at moderate intensity, chassis build quality, acoustic output, price-to-performance ratio, and storage footprint — because a rower that folds into a closet gets used; one parked in the middle of a room often doesn't.

products.review: The Concept2 Model D is the global benchmark for a reason: air resistance scales infinitely with effort, so a beginner and an Olympic rower get an honest workout on the same machine. The PM5 monitor's data accuracy is trusted by coaches worldwide. The real trade-off is noise — air rowers are loud, and if you share walls with neighbors you will hear about it.
pros: PM5 monitor is the industry data standard

products.review: The Wave pairs electromagnetic resistance with a 16-inch touchscreen and a genuinely motivated class library — the feel is smoother and quieter than air, which makes it the better apartment choice. The subscription cost (~$44/month) is real and ongoing; factor it into the total ownership price before buying.
pros: 16-inch screen with live and on-demand classes

products.review: The RW900's 22-inch rotating HD screen is the largest in this comparison and iFIT's global route library is genuinely deep. Twenty-six digital resistance levels adjust automatically during instructor workouts. At 218 lbs it is semi-permanent once placed, and the iFIT subscription adds ongoing cost.
cons: Heavy at 218 lbs — placement is semi-permanent

products.review: Ergatta's water flywheel produces the most natural rowing sensation in this group — the sound and resistance closely mimic open-water rowing. Its game-based workout platform is a genuine motivator for solo athletes who find instructor workouts repetitive. The proprietary platform limits you to Ergatta's ecosystem.
pros: Water flywheel = most realistic rowing feel

products.review: At around $350, the SF-RW5515 gives you 12 magnetic resistance levels, a quiet ride, and a no-subscription entry into rowing. It won't replicate the feel of air or water, but for building a rowing habit or testing whether you like the movement, it's the lowest-risk starting point in this category.
pros: 12 resistance levels on a solid aluminum rail
cons: Resistance feel is less dynamic than air or water

recommendedFor.label: For serious home athletes
recommendedFor.reason: Industry-standard data accuracy and infinite resistance scaling reward athletes who want to track real progress over years, not months.

recommendedFor.label: For apartment dwellers
recommendedFor.reason: Electromagnetic resistance and a folding frame keep noise and footprint low enough for shared-wall living.

recommendedFor.label: For content-driven motivation
recommendedFor.reason: The 22-inch screen and iFIT library suit people who need variety and instructor accountability to stay consistent.

recommendedFor.label: For solo competitive athletes
recommendedFor.reason: Game-based racing against personal records works better than class-based content for self-motivated athletes who dislike group instruction.

recommendedFor.label: For rowing beginners on a budget
recommendedFor.reason: A quiet, affordable machine with no subscription commitment is the right tool for testing whether rowing fits your routine.
